topic: New flagship topic: critical-overlap tail exponent for sharp partial identification of treatment effects under positivity failure. Pre-anchor check: closest published anchors are Khan-Tamer 2010 on irregular identification/support and inverse weighting, Rothe-style robust inference under limited overlap, Hong-Leung-Li finite-population limited-overlap inference, and recent non-overlap ATE bounds. Our theorem is not that because it is not an estimator or trimming rule and not the generic statement that non-overlap widens Manski bounds: the focal object is an overlap-tail profile alpha(P) and an extremal-law certificate showing an exact phase threshold for the sharp identified-set width of CATT/ATE under bounded outcomes and observed covariate-propensity tail mass. Require a concrete nonroutine object: a finite/two-tail witness family with the same nominal overlap support but different polynomial tail exponents, an explicit endpoint-attaining sequence, and an iff threshold separating finite-width, polynomial blow-up, and vacuous regimes. If the proposal reduces to ordinary support coverage, overlap weights, Manski missing-cell bounds, trimming, or generic LP duality without the exponent witness and endpoint certificate, pivot or stop early.

open_problems.open_problem: Find the exact tail-exponent threshold for sharp CATT or ATE identified-set width under bounded outcomes when positivity fails only through propensity-score tails rather than through nominal support loss.

source_refs.claim: Khan and Tamer show that inverse-weight point identification can be irregular, with convergence rates depending on relative tail conditions.
source_refs.author: Khan and Tamer

source_refs.claim: Heiler and Kazak characterize alpha-stable limits for IPW/DR inference with many extreme propensity scores, but not sharp identified-set width.
source_refs.author: Heiler and Kazak

source_refs.claim: Susmann, McClean, and Diaz derive non-overlap ATE bounds whose width is proportional to the non-overlap subpopulation size.
source_refs.author: Susmann, McClean, and Diaz

source_refs.excerpt: critical overlap exponent controlling interval-width growth near e(x)=0 or 1
This run should not be read as a mathematical rejection of the overlap-exponent topic.

open_problems.why_unsolved: The web literature splits tail behavior into estimator-rate and inference results, while partial-identification work gives bounds driven by non-overlap mass or pooling rules; the closest in-repo overlap-exponent attempt failed before theorem review and left no banked witness or proof resolving how alpha(P) controls interval-width growth near e(x)=0 or 1.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to classify support as covered or uncovered fails because the prior README frames the unresolved object as an exponent controlling interval-width growth, and Susmann-McClean-Diaz bounds depend on non-overlap mass rather than distinguishing laws with the same nominal support and different propensity-tail exponents.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp identified-set width theorem giving an iff phase threshold in alpha(P), with finite-width, polynomial blow-up, and vacuous regimes for CATT or ATE under bounded outcomes.

open_problems.open_problem: Give an endpoint-attaining or endpoint-approaching witness for tail-driven vacuity of sharp CATE bounds that is mathematically compatible with the stated moment or tail restrictions.

source_refs.excerpt: Repair the converse witness by exhibiting an actual untruncated non-integrable law
The weak-star-open vacuity class has no concrete non-corner law

open_problems.why_unsolved: Prior reviewers rejected the attempted heavy-tail phase theorem because the claimed vacuity or converse witness was not a concrete law in the asserted class: finite truncations could show sequence divergence, but not the stated infinite-width law in a neighborhood.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt truncated-Pareto approximation fails because the reviewer notes finite truncations cannot themselves satisfy W_Gamma(P')=+infty and prove only divergence along a sequence.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A converse sharpness certificate consisting of an explicit untruncated law or a formally downgraded endpoint-approaching sequence, with a regime statement specifying when each certificate is valid.

open_problems.open_problem: Identify the correct phase boundary for finite sharp CATE width under tail restrictions, separating undefined-mean, finite-width, and vacuity regimes without treating alpha=1 as automatically vacuous.

source_refs.excerpt: the true boundary is not 'alpha=1 gives vacuity'
Restate the flagship threshold around finite first absolute moment

open_problems.why_unsolved: The prior proposal's alpha=1 phase-transition claim was rejected as internally false: reviewers said alpha<1 may leave means undefined without an L1 convention, while alpha=1 with a finite first absolute moment can still give finite bounds.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt alpha-moment dichotomy fails because the reviewer says finite alpha<1 moments do not ensure the CATE mean exists, while finite alpha=1 moments can restore finite width.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An iff phase theorem over tail classes that separately states well-posedness of the estimand, finiteness of the sharp interval, and any vacuity or blow-up threshold.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct endpoint-attaining extremal distributions showing how two overlap tails with the same nominal support can have different sharp treatment-effect widths because their tail exponents differ.

source_refs.claim: Lee and Weidner bound ATE and ATT under violated overlap by interpolating between Manski bounds and inverse-propensity weighting through limited pooling.
source_refs.author: Lee and Weidner

source_refs.claim: Manski gives nonparametric treatment-effect bounds when exact identification fails, providing the no-pooling benchmark for missing counterfactual cells.
source_refs.author: Manski

source_refs.claim: Susmann, McClean, and Diaz add a trimmed overlap effect to worst-case bounded-outcome contributions in non-overlap regions.
source_refs.author: Susmann, McClean, and Diaz

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing sharp bounds either use coarse missing-cell or Manski extremes, or impose a limited-pooling architecture; neither supplies a two-tail extremal-law certificate that keeps nominal overlap support fixed while varying only polynomial tail thickness.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to invoke generic linear-programming sharpness fails because Lee and Weidner restrict the admissible pooling structure while Manski-style endpoints ignore the continuous tail geometry that determines the proposed exponent.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: An explicit finite or two-tail witness family plus an endpoint-attaining sequence proving that the proposed tail-exponent bound is sharp.

open_problems.open_problem: Separate the one-sided CATT or ATT tail problem from the two-sided ATE tail problem by characterizing when a bad treated or untreated propensity tail alone makes the sharp identified interval finite, divergent, or vacuous.

source_refs.claim: Hong, Leung, and Li define limited overlap by propensity scores degenerating to zero or one and show convergence slows with n min_x p_n(x)(1-p_n(x)).
source_refs.author: Hong, Leung, and Li

source_refs.claim: Lee and Weidner construct bounds on both ATE and ATT that remain valid when the overlap condition is violated.
source_refs.author: Lee and Weidner

source_refs.claim: Rothe attributes distorted inference under limited overlap to small local sample sizes in regions of weak overlap.
source_refs.author: Rothe

open_problems.why_unsolved: Hong, Leung, and Li formalize degenerating finite-population strata and rates, while Lee and Weidner give ATE/ATT bounds under limited pooling; the literature has not isolated a sharp one-tail CATT/ATT width threshold under bounded outcomes and superpopulation propensity-tail mass.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to replace p(1-p) by the treated-tail mass fails because Hong, Leung, and Li study convergence rates for finite-population estimators, not sharp identified intervals for missing potential outcomes in a one-sided tail.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A one-sided tail theorem for CATT or ATT, paired with a two-sided ATE theorem, identifying exactly which tail exponents enter each sharp width formula.

literature_map:
Khan and Tamer (2010, Econometrica) are the closest tail anchor: inverse-weight point identification can be irregular and convergence depends on relative tail conditions.
Rothe (2017, Econometrica) shows limited overlap harms confidence intervals through small local sample sizes, but the object is inference, not sharp partial-ID width.
Hong, Leung, and Li (2020, Econometrics Journal) formalize limited overlap as finite-population propensity degeneration and derive slower rates based on effective sample size.
Heiler and Kazak (2021, Journal of Econometrics) use regular variation and alpha-stable limits for IPW/DR inference with many extreme propensity scores.
Sasaki and Ura (2022, Econometric Theory) and Ma and Wang (2020, JASA) study robust inference for moments of ratios or IPW with small denominators and trimming choices.
Crump, Hotz, Imbens, and Mitnik (2009, Biometrika) give the canonical trimming/subpopulation response to limited overlap, explicitly shifting the estimand.
Li, Morgan, and Zaslavsky (2018, JASA) give overlap weights, again targeting the overlap population rather than the full population effect.
Manski (1990, AER) supplies the foundational no-assumption treatment-effect bounds; Imbens and Manski (2004, Econometrica) address confidence intervals for interval-identified parameters.
Nethery, Mealli, and Dominici (2019, Annals of Applied Statistics) emphasize that non-overlap makes population inference rely on extrapolation unless the estimand changes.
Lee and Weidner (2026, Journal of Econometrics) provide new ATE/ATT bounds robust to violated overlap by interpolating between Manski bounds and IPW through limited pooling.
Susmann, McClean, and Diaz (2025/2026 arXiv) derive bounded-outcome non-overlap ATE bounds, with width proportional to non-overlap mass and smooth estimable approximations.
The study-pipeline bundle was checked as augmentation; its Manski level-set and interval-regression questions are adjacent partial-ID substrate but do not supply the required overlap-tail exponent gap.
The live gap is that published work has tail exponents for estimator rates and mass/pooling bounds for partial ID, but not a sharp phase law for identified-set width certified by exponent-varying extremal witnesses.
prior_proposal_map: Mined CausalSmith/doc/formalization only. Highest-signal adjacent source was _bank/failed/pid_critical_overlap_exponent_v1/README.md: same overlap-exponent topic, failed before proposal/review because of Stage -1.1 JSON/pipeline issues; reusable artifact is only a literature map. Main reviewer-prescribed gaps came from _bank/failed/pid_heavy_tail_cate_bounds_v1 reviews angle2_v1, angle3_v1, and angle4_v1: repair moment-feasible extremal law, correct false alpha=1/vacuity boundary, repair converse witness, and anchor against published Gamma-selection bounds without mischaracterizing real-valued coverage. Inspected rejected proposal angles for pid_heavy_tail_cate_bounds_v1 and pid_ot_dte_marginal_v1; the latter yields an overlap-envelope DTE path sharpness gap but is less central to the requested CATT/ATE overlap-tail exponent. Bank READMEs reviewed: pid_critical_overlap_exponent_v1 and pid_heavy_tail_cate_bounds_v1. Study-pipeline gaps were considered as augmentation; they mainly concern LATE, Manski level-set restrictions, and interval-regression inference, so they were not emitted as overlap-tail open problems.
